questions.question: What property distinguishes |Φ⁺⟩ and |Φ⁻⟩ from |Ψ⁺⟩ and |Ψ⁻⟩?
questions.explanation: |Φ±⟩ states have even parity (contain |00⟩ and |11⟩ - same bits), while |Ψ±⟩ states have odd parity (contain |01⟩ and |10⟩ - different bits). This parity is measurable and helps distinguish between Bell state pairs.

questions.question: How can |Φ⁺⟩ be transformed into |Ψ⁺⟩ using local operations?

options.text: Apply Z gate to one qubit
Apply X gate to one qubit
Apply H gate to one qubit
Cannot be done with local operations

questions.explanation: Applying X to one qubit flips that qubit: |Φ⁺⟩ = (|00⟩+|11⟩)/√2 → (|01⟩+|10⟩)/√2 = |Ψ⁺⟩. This shows Bell states can be converted using single-qubit operations, demonstrating they have the same entanglement.

questions.question: What is the maximum Bell state measurement success rate with linear optics alone?
questions.explanation: Linear optics (beam splitters, wave plates) can only distinguish |Ψ⁺⟩ and |Ψ⁻⟩ via Hong-Ou-Mandel interference. The |Φ±⟩ states produce ambiguous outputs. Complete BSM requires nonlinear optics, hyperentanglement, or auxiliary photons.

questions.question: In quantum teleportation, how many classical bits must Alice send to Bob?
questions.explanation: Alice's BSM produces one of four outcomes (2 bits of information). She must send these 2 bits so Bob knows which Pauli correction (I, X, Z, or XZ) to apply. Without this, Bob's qubit is maximally mixed regardless of the input state.

questions.question: What is the correlation ⟨ZZ⟩ for the Bell state |Ψ⁻⟩ = (|01⟩ - |10⟩)/√2?
questions.explanation: For |Ψ⁻⟩, measuring both qubits in Z always gives opposite results (01 or 10), so ⟨ZZ⟩ = -1. This anti-correlation in the computational basis is characteristic of the singlet state.

questions.question: In superdense coding, how many classical bits can be transmitted using one qubit plus shared entanglement?
questions.explanation: With shared entanglement, Alice encodes 2 bits by applying one of {I, X, Z, XZ} to her qubit, transforming the shared |Φ⁺⟩ into one of the four Bell states. Bob performs BSM to decode. This doubles classical capacity of a qubit channel.

questions.question: Bell states can be prepared from product states using only local operations and classical communication (LOCC).
questions.explanation: False. Creating entanglement from separable states requires a non-local (entangling) operation like CNOT. LOCC can transform one Bell state into another, but cannot create entanglement from nothing. This is why entanglement is a valuable resource.
